channel.title: Articles on Academis Demo
channel.description: Recent content in Articles on Academis Demo

item.title: Evidence standards in contested historical claims
Critical Thinking as Infrastructure
item.description: What critical thinking actually means Critical thinking is not a synonym for scepticism or contrarianism. It means the capacity to assess the quality of an argument regardless of its source — to distinguish claims supported by evidence from claims grounded in opinion, and to recognise when a conclusion does not follow from its premises. In educational practice, this translates into concrete skills: identifying hidden assumptions, evaluating the relevance of evidence, detecting errors in reasoning, and constructing honest counter-arguments.

item.title: The Civic Cost of Platform Monopolies
item.description: The infrastructure argument We do not allow a single company to own the roads, the water pipes, and the electricity grid simultaneously. The reason is not aesthetic or accidental — it is that infrastructure monopolies create structural power over everything that depends on them, and the potential for abuse is too great to ignore. Digital information infrastructure is now in that category. Search engines and social feeds are not luxury products; they are the primary means by which citizens in most democracies locate news, evaluate candidates, and form political opinions.

item.title: The Unfinished Argument for Urban Density
item.description: The evidence in brief The core claims are not seriously disputed in the urban economics literature. Dense urban areas: Emit significantly less carbon per capita than sprawling suburbs, primarily through transport mode shift and shared-wall heating efficiency Generate higher productivity through agglomeration effects — the density of human interaction in a city is not incidental to its economic output, it is a primary cause Cost less per resident to serve with infrastructure: roads, water, sewerage, and emergency services all have lower per-capita unit costs in denser configurations Correlate with lower rates of cardiovascular disease, partly through walking rates and partly through reduced air pollution from shorter vehicle trips The debate is not about whether these effects are real.

item.title: The Science-to-Headline Pipeline and Where It Breaks
item.description: The chain of transformation A peer-reviewed finding travels roughly as follows before reaching a general audience: The research is conducted and submitted A university or institution issues a press release on publication Science journalists or general reporters cover the press release, usually without reading the paper Editors write headlines optimised for engagement The story is shared on social platforms, often stripped of its headline context The headline circulates as a received fact Each of these stages involves incentives that push representation away from accuracy.

item.title: Why Evidence Hierarchies Break Down in Policy Debates
item.description: Where the hierarchy comes from The evidence hierarchy was developed in the context of clinical medicine, specifically for evaluating the effectiveness of interventions on individual patients. Randomised controlled trials sit at the top because random assignment eliminates selection bias — the most common distortion in medical research. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses sit higher still because they pool results across trials, reducing the influence of any single study’s noise. This framework has been enormously productive in medicine.

item.title: What Translation Loses and What That Means for History
item.description: The problem no one talks about The dominant model of historical scholarship is built on direct engagement with primary sources. The historian goes to the archive, reads the documents, forms an interpretation. The authority of the argument rests, to a significant degree, on this direct encounter with the original material. What this model does not adequately account for is how much of the work is done in translation — formally, where researchers rely on translated collections; informally, where researchers with some language competence read difficult texts with the help of dictionaries, colleagues, and intuition; and structurally, where an entire historiographical tradition about a culture is built on the work of a small number of scholars who actually had the relevant linguistic competence, and the rest inherit their interpretations.

item.title: Print and the Disruption of Epistemic Authority in Sixteenth-Century Europe
item.description: The information-market argument The dominant historiography of the Reformation-era information revolution has emphasised the content of printed controversy — Lutheran pamphlets, Catholic apologetics, satirical woodcuts. This article argues that the more consequential shift was structural: print changed the cost structure of credibility production and distribution in ways that systematically disadvantaged incumbents. Pre-print knowledge institutions — the church, the university, the notarial network — derived part of their authority from their control of expensive knowledge infrastructure.

item.title: Building an Editorial Style Guide for Independent Publications
item.description: What a style guide is actually for Style guides have two distinct functions that are usually conflated. The first is consistency — ensuring that the same word is spelled the same way, that dates follow the same format, that measurements use the same system. This is the function most guides emphasise, and it is genuinely useful. The second function is more important and less often served: editorial codification. A good style guide records what the publication has decided about contested questions — questions about evidence standards, the treatment of disputed claims, the use of anonymous sources, the relationship between criticism and fairness — in enough detail that editors and writers can apply those decisions consistently without relitigating them every time.

item.title: Transparency Requirements for Automated Decision Systems in Public Administration
item.description: The accountability gap Traditional administrative accountability rests on a chain of human decisions that can, in principle, be traced, explained, and challenged. A civil servant makes a decision; the decision is recorded; an appeal mechanism exists; a superior can be identified. Automated decision systems do not eliminate this chain, but they create several new failure modes within it: Opacity of the model — the decision procedure may not be interpretable even by the administering authority Distributed responsibility — the model was built by a vendor, trained on historic data that reflects past human decisions, and deployed by a department whose staff may not understand its operation Scale effects — a single miscalibrated model can affect millions of citizens before any individual challenge reveals the error Scope of this analysis

item.title: Civic Memory Archive
item.description: Why this existed The Northgate industrial district was built between 1930 and 1960 to house light manufacturing — machine shops, print works, small-batch fabrication. By 1990, most of the original industrial use had left. By 2000, the buildings had been informally subdivided, extended, and repurposed by a range of occupants: artist studios, small workshops, storage, light commercial, a few live-work conversions that technically violated planning conditions. This kind of fabric is architecturally unremarkable and historically important.

item.title: Open Urban Documentation Initiative
item.description: Why this project exists Informal housing — self-built, incrementally expanded, legally ambiguous — is the dominant form of housing for a significant portion of the urban population globally, including in cities that do not normally appear in discussions of the “global south.” Post-industrial cities in Eastern and Southern Europe have substantial informal settlements whose built history is almost entirely undocumented in any accessible form. This creates a specific kind of institutional blindness: planning authorities make decisions about these areas based on maps that do not reflect what is actually built; residents cannot access formal credit because their homes do not appear in cadastral records; and the accumulated knowledge of how communities have solved housing problems with minimal resources is lost when settlements are demolished or regularised.
